title: Polymer clay faux-marble bowl
subtitle: A small decorative bowl built from marbled strips draped into a baking dish
excerpt: A small decorative bowl built from twisted strips of two polymer clay colours marbled together, draped into a small oven-safe baking dish as the form, and baked rigid. The finished bowl is around 8 cm across and holds dry trinkets only. Polymer clay is not food-safe.
sourceNotes: Marbled and mokume-gane techniques in polymer clay are contemporary studio adaptations of earlier metalworking methods. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: PVC-based modelling material that sets in a domestic oven at 110-130°C. Decorative use only; not food-safe even after curing.
The heat process that sets polymer clay in a domestic oven. The clay goes in soft and comes out rigid.
Twisting two or more clay colours together so they produce the veined patterning of natural marble. Twist too much and the colours blend; twist too little and the pattern reads as stripes.

content.text: A small marbled bowl is a single-session project. Two colours of
polymer clay

are twisted together into marbled strips, the strips are laid across each other on the inside of a small oven-safe baking dish, and the whole thing bakes at 110°C until the clay sets rigid. The dish acts as the mould; the clay takes the dish's curve. The finished bowl is around 8 cm across, holds dry trinkets (rings, hair clips, small change), and is decorative only.

attrs.title: Baking polymer clay
attrs.body: Use a dedicated oven tray and dedicated baking dish. Verify oven temperature with an oven thermometer. Do not share with food cookware after polymer use.

items.name: Polymer clay, dark accent (charcoal grey, deep blue, or terracotta)
Small oven-safe baking dish (8 cm diameter, ~3 cm deep)
items.substitution: A small ramekin works; dedicate it to polymer use

content.text: Conditioning the clay

Squeeze and roll each colour separately in the hands for one or two minutes. The clay should soften enough that a rolled snake bends in a U without breaking. Conditioned clay holds detail and joins cleanly; cold clay crumbles.
Marbling the clay
1. Roll two snakes
Roll the white clay into a snake 12 mm thick and 20 cm long. Roll the dark accent into a thinner snake (6 mm thick and 20 cm long). Lay the dark snake on top of the white.
2. Twist into marble

Hold one end of the paired snakes in each hand and twist gently in opposite directions, so the two snakes spiral around each other like a rope. Fold the twisted rope in half and roll back into a single snake. Twist again, fold, roll. Repeat three or four times total.
Marbling
is a balance: stop when the dark clay runs through the white in clear veins. Twist too many times and the two colours blend to a uniform grey.
3. Cut the strips

Roll the marbled snake flat under the rolling pin to a sheet 4 mm thick. The veining stretches as the sheet rolls; this is what gives the bowl its long marble streaks. Cut the sheet into long strips around 15 mm wide. Aim for six to eight strips of varying length.
Lining the dish
1. Prepare the dish

Line the inside of the baking dish with a single piece of greaseproof paper, pressed against the curve. The paper prevents the clay from sticking to the dish and allows clean release after baking. Crumple the paper first then smooth it back out: it conforms to the curve better when slightly creased.
2. Lay the strips

Lay the marbled strips across the inside of the dish in a star pattern, from rim to rim, crossing at the centre. Press each strip gently into the curve of the dish as it goes in. Aim for a wall thickness of around 4 mm (one strip thick). Where the strips cross at the centre, the bowl base will be slightly thicker; this is structural and helps the bowl sit flat once released.
3. Fill the gaps and smooth

Press the strip edges together where they meet inside the dish, working from the centre outward. Fill any gaps with small offcuts of the marbled clay. Smooth the inside surface with the wooden rib, working gently so the marble pattern stays visible. The smoother the inside, the more refined the bowl reads.
4. Trim the rim
Trim any clay overhanging the dish rim with the craft knife so the bowl rim sits flush with the dish rim. Smooth the cut edge with a damp finger.
Baking
Place the dish (with the bowl lining inside) onto the dedicated baking tray. Check the oven temperature with the oven thermometer; aim for 110°C (fan) and verify. Bake for 30 minutes for a 4 mm wall.
Curing
sets the clay rigid in the curved shape of the dish. Underbaked clay snaps when handled; overbaked clay browns and goes brittle.
Removing and finishing

Let the dish cool for ten minutes before handling. Lift the bowl out of the dish (the greaseproof paper helps release; peel it away from the bowl after lifting). Sand the rim with 600-grit wet-and-dry sandpaper to smooth any rough edges. Polish the inside and outside surfaces by rubbing with a soft cloth, then a paper towel. Brush on a thin coat of acrylic gloss varnish for higher shine; a matte varnish keeps the surface looking more like natural stone.
Care

Polymer clay is not food-safe. The bowl holds dry small items: rings, hair clips, small change, paperclips. Do not use as a soap dish, water vessel, or kitchen-side dish. Wipe clean with a barely damp cloth; the cured clay is water-resistant but prolonged soaking can soften the surface.
Variations
Three-colour marble: swap one colour for two close shades. This gives a more layered look (white with pale grey and charcoal, for instance).
Larger bowl: 80 g white plus 25 g accent fills a 10 cm dish; bake time increases to 35 minutes for the thicker walls.
Use a square-base baking dish instead of a round one: the marbled strips stack and the finished bowl has straight sides instead of curves.

items.symptom: Marble pattern blurred to a uniform grey
items.cause: Twisted and folded too many times
items.fix: Roll fresh clay; twist and fold three or four times maximum. Stop while the dark colour still shows as clear veins

items.symptom: Bowl will not release from the dish after baking
items.cause: Greaseproof paper omitted, or the paper torn during pressing
items.fix: Run a thin blade between the paper and the dish; replace the paper with a fresh piece on the next attempt and press it gently against the curve without tearing

items.symptom: Crack runs through the bowl wall after baking
items.cause: Strips not pressed firmly together; the join between strips opened during the bake
items.fix: Press strip edges firmly together before baking; fill gaps with small offcuts of the same marbled clay; smooth the inside with the wooden rib

items.cause: Strips uneven thickness; wall pulled inward where thinner during the bake
items.fix: Roll the marbled sheet to a consistent 4 mm thickness; the rolling pin pressed against slat guides keeps the sheet even
